(To cause the photoelectric effect) the energy (of a photon) must be greater than the work function (of the photocathode) Or (to cause the photoelectric effect) the frequency (of a photon) must be greater than the threshold frequency (of the photocathode) Photon energy is proportional to frequency, so photons below a certain frequency / energy cannot cause the photoelectric effect (1) (1) −19 J Use of E = hf Use of v = fλ Comparison of 840 nm with 700 nm and conclusion all wavelengths of visible light can be detected Or Comparison of 2.85 × 10 -19 J with 2.37 × 10 −19 J and conclusion all wavelengths of visible light can be detected Or Comparison of 1.78 eV with 1.48 eV and conclusion all wavelengths of visible light can be detected Or Comparison of 3.57 x 10 Hz with 4.29 x 10 Hz Example calculation E = 1.48 eV × 1.6 × 10 −19 J eV −1 = 2.37 × 10 −19 J f threshold = 2.37 × 10 −19 J 6.63 × 10 −34 Js =3.57×10 Hz λ threshold = 3 × 10 m s −1 3.57 × 10 Hz = 840 × 10 −9 m 700 nm < 840 nm so all wavelengths of visible light can be detected (1) (1) (1) (1) k = ½ mv Use of 푉 = 푊 푄 Final kinetic energy = 1.81 × 10 −17 (J) Example calculation Initial E k = 0.5×9.11×10 −31 kg× ( 1.35×10 m s −1 ) =8.30×10 −19 J W = 108 V × 1.6 × 10 −19 C = 1.73 × 10 −17 J Final kinetic energy (= 1.73 × 10 −17 J + 8.30 × 10 −19 J) = 1.81 × 10 −17 J (1) (1) (1) Use of V = IR to calculate current in circuit Calculates total p.d across resistors A to D Use of sum of e.m.f. = sum of p.d Internal resistance = 1.20 MΩ OR Calculates total p.d. across resistors A to D Uses ratio of resistance = ratio of p.d.
